summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, Democracy / anti-fascism, CS education / AI policy. The strongest items in this run are Middle East crisis live: US and Iran in blockade stalemate as Washington’s navy secretary leaves office ‘immediately’, Thursday news quiz: insurance scams, drinking games and errors of biblical proportions, and How to find a career you love – for gen Z and everyone else: ‘You don’t want your life’s compass to be dread’.

mainItems.title: Middle East crisis live: US and Iran in blockade stalemate as Washington’s navy secretary leaves office ‘immediately’
mainItems.summary: Iran says two seized ships transferred to its coast; US navy secretary exiting post ‘effective immediately’, says Pentagon ‘Impossible’ to reopen strait of Hormuz amid ‘flagrant’ ceasefire breaches, Iran says The Pentagon abruptly…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Thursday news quiz: insurance scams, drinking games and errors of biblical proportions
mainItems.summary: Test yourself on topical news trivia, pop culture and general knowledge every Thursday.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: How to find a career you love – for gen Z and everyone else: ‘You don’t want your life’s compass to be dread’
mainItems.summary: In her new book, New York Times investigative journalist Jodi Kantor has set her mind to helping young people find their life’s work.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: ‘Lawrence is karma’: the gangster who became an icon of Modi’s India
mainItems.summary: Lawrence Bishnoi has been in high-security custody for more than a decade. During that time, he has been linked to multiple high-profile killings, both in India and as far afield as Canada.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Czech journalists threaten to strike over plan to scrap licence fees
mainItems.summary: Government aims to move TV and radio funding under state control, which critics say undermines independence Journalists at the Czech Republic’s public broadcasters have said they are prepared to go on strike unless the government of the…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: OpenAI now lets teams make custom bots that can do work on their own
mainItems.summary: OpenAI is giving users of its Business, Enterprise, Edu, and Teachers plans access to cloud-based "workspace" agents available in ChatGPT that can perform business tasks.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Opinion: How New York City Can Offer Schools That Are Both Integrated and Rigorous
mainItems.summary: Each summer during new employee orientation at Prospect Schools, I open with a hard reality: Despite the landmark 1954 Supreme Court ruling in Brown v. Board of Education, most school districts remain deeply segregated.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: Ecco the Dolphin: Complete announced, includes remasters of the first two entries and brand-new game
mainItems.summary: As a millennial, I've always had a soft spot for the original Ecco the Dolphin games (not so much for the latter entries), so it was great to learn a while back a brand-new project is in development .
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
mainItems.summary: While sourdough starter might conjure images of crusty artisan loaves , that only scratches the surface of naturally leavened breads.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Lower-stakes, but high fit for the maker/craft side of your brain.

mainItems.title: Tesla's making money. But it's planning to spend an awful lot more
mainItems.summary: Tesla's profits were up from this time last year. But the company warned investors to prepare for expensive investments in next-generation technology like humanoid robots and AI.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Interesting where engineering, science, and tech infrastructure collide.

mainItems.title: Gene therapy for a rare type of deafness shows lasting results
mainItems.summary: Researchers say a gene therapy allowed deaf children and adults as old as 32 to hear for the first time. The benefits have persisted for more than two years for some patients.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.

highlights.title: Trump’s presidency is what evil looks like: absurd, frightening, cruel
highlights.notes: Particularly strong Guardian opinion piece, noted for future briefing highlights.
